rules.rule_statement: For the plaintiff in a tort action, the focus is retrospective; the goal is to place the plaintiff in the same position he was in before the tort. Damages include compensation for past and present harms as well as future harms (e.g., corrective surgery, lost wages) that will be suffered.

rules.canonical_name: Physical And Emotional Consequences (Pain And Suffering)
rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff is entitled to compensation for the impairment or loss of physical abilities such as movement or sight, other bodily harm (e.g., damage to an internal organ), illness, and emotional distress caused by fear, anxiety, humiliation, or loss of freedom. Generically, such damages are labeled “pain and suffering.” The plaintiff need not suffer a pecuniary loss to be entitled to damages for these consequences; hence, they are frequently referred to as “noneconomic damages.” A plaintiff must be able to experience pain to recover for it. However, even a plaintiff in a vegetative state may be aware of pain (e.g., a comatose patient who responds to a pinprick by moaning) despite not being conscious in a more general sense. While an award of medical expenses is generally accompanied by a pain-and-suffering award, the two types of damages are not necessarily linked. An award of medical expenses does not automatically mandate a pain-and-suffering award. A plaintiff may recover for the loss of enjoyment of life (i.e., hedonic loss). However, about half of the states require that this loss be treated within the scope of pain-and-suffering damages, while the other half permit separate consideration of this loss. Damages for emotional distress are always allowed as ancillary damages when the plaintiff suffers physical injury that gives rise to a cause of action (e.g., assault, battery) or if the defendant’s action is intentional or outrageous. Limits may be imposed, however, on the recovery of damages solely for emotional distress arising from the defendant’s negligent conduct. Actual damages may include damages for emotional distress, depending on the particular context in which the term appears. Emotional distress damages are not available as actual damages under the Privacy Act.

rules.rule_statement: Formula Compensatory damages = (expectation damages + consequential damages + incidental damages) − mitigation Alternatively, the plaintiff might recover liquidated damages, reliance damages, or restitutionary relief. Formula When a seller breaches a contract for goods: Buyer’s damages = (market price − contract price) + (incidental damages + consequential damages − expenses saved) The time of measuring the market price is the time that the buyer learns of the breach. Alternatively, the buyer may buy substitute goods (i.e., cover); such a buyer is entitled to the difference between the replacement cost (rather than the market price) and the contract price. If the buyer accepts nonconforming goods, then the buyer may pursue a breach-of-warranty action through which the buyer can recover the difference between the value of the goods accepted and the value of such goods had they been as warranted. Value is determined as of the date of acceptance. Incidental and consequential damages may be recovered as well. If the buyer justifiably revokes acceptance with respect to received goods, then the buyer, in addition to recovering the market–contract differential, may recover any amount paid to the seller for the goods. In addition, the buyer has not only a security interest in any such goods for the amount paid but also the right to sell such goods. Restatement (Second) of Torts (hereinafter “R2d Torts”) §§ 901–903, 905–906, 911, 913A. R2d Torts §§ 905, 924(a). FAA v. Cooper, 132 S. Ct. 1441 (2012). Restatement (Second) of Contracts (hereinafter “R2d Contracts”) §§ 344–347. UCC §§ 2-711 to -13. UCC § 2?714. UCC § 2-711.

rules.rule_statement: In a contract action, as an alternative to compensatory damages, a plaintiff may be awarded monetary damages based on her reliance interest. The plaintiff can recover expenditures made in preparation for performing the contract, as well as expenditures made in performing the contract. Rule Reliance damages are generally awarded only when the plaintiff cannot prove a profit would have been made on the contract or when the contract would have produced a loss. Reliance damages cannot exceed the contract price. R2d Contracts §§ 344, 349.

Reliance Damages
In a contract action, as an alternative to compensatory damages, a plaintiff may be awarded monetary damages based on her reliance interest. The plaintiff can recover expenditures made in preparation for performing the contract, as well as expenditures made in performing the contract. Rule Reliance damages are generally awarded only when the plaintiff cannot prove a profit would have been made on the contract or when the contract would have produced a loss. Reliance damages cannot exceed the contract price. R2d Contracts §§ 344, 349.

rules.rule_statement: In a contract action, as another alternative to compensatory damages, a plaintiff may be awarded monetary damages based on his restitutionary interest. If there is a total breach of the contract by the defendant, then damages are measured by the benefit conferred on the defendant by the plaintiff. Remember, instead of a suit based on the contract, a plaintiff may elect to bring a suit based on the unjust enrichment of the defendant, for which restitutionary remedies, including monetary payments, are available. When relevant to the issue at hand, you should discuss both restitution and compensatory damages on the exam. R2d Contracts §§ 344, 373; Restatement (Third) of Restitution and Unjust Enrichment (hereinafter “R3d Restitution”) § 49.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Nominal damages may be awarded when a plaintiff has established the elements of a cause of action but has not suffered harm or loss (or the harm or loss is slight). These damages are often for a trivial sum of money (e.g., $1). Such an award can, by establishing the plaintiff’s rights, have the effect of a declaratory judgment or entitle the plaintiff to recover litigation costs from the defendant (if the prevailing party is entitled to such recovery). Nominal damages are not available in causes of action (such as negligence and fraud) that require the plaintiff to establish damages. R2d Torts § 907; R2d Contracts § 346(2).

rules.canonical_name: Ratio Between Compensatory And Punitive Damages
rules.rule_statement: While there is not a bright-line test for punitive damages, an award that exceeds a single-digit ratio between punitive and compensatory damages will rarely comply with the Due Process Clause. Moreover, if a compensatory damages award is substantial, then a punitive damages award in excess of the compensatory award may be grossly excessive.

rules.canonical_name: Defendant’S Wealth And Excessiveness Of Punitive Damages
rules.rule_statement: The defendant’s wealth is an important consideration in determining the excessiveness of a punitive damages award. The defendant’s net worth is the most common measure of a defendant’s financial condition but is subject to manipulation and should not be the only permissible standard for determining whether punitive damages are excessive in relation to that condition.

rules.rule_statement: For damages to be awarded, the defendant must have caused the plaintiff’s injury. This causation requirement is generally stated as a but-for test; that is, but for the defendant’s behavior, the plaintiff would not have suffered injury. However, other tests may be applied, such as in situations in which the plaintiff’s injury has causes in addition to the defendant’s behavior.

However, other tests may be applied, such as in situations in which the plaintiff’s injury has causes in addition to the defendant’s behavior.

rules.rule_statement: In a negligence action, a plaintiff, in addition to establishing factual causation, must prove legal (i.e., proximate) causation as an element of the plaintiff’s case. Legal causation is based on foreseeability. A defendant is liable only for those harms that were reasonably foreseeable when the defendant breached his duty. If a plaintiff’s harm arises from a risk that the defendant could not have reasonably foreseen, then the defendant is not liable for such harms.

Foreseeability—Negligence
In a negligence action, a plaintiff, in addition to establishing factual causation, must prove legal (i.e., proximate) causation as an element of the plaintiff’s case. Legal causation is based on foreseeability. A defendant is liable only for those harms that were reasonably foreseeable when the defendant breached his duty. If a plaintiff’s harm arises from a risk that the defendant could not have reasonably foreseen, then the defendant is not liable for such harms.

rules.rule_statement: Consequential damages are reasonably foreseeable losses to a nonbreaching party that go beyond expectation damages, such as lost profits. Consequential damages are recoverable if they were the natural and probable consequences of the breach, if they were “in the contemplation of the parties at the time the contract was made,” or if they were otherwise foreseeable. Example: A computer manufacturer breaches a contract to deliver computers to a brokerage firm for use in the firm’s business. The computer manufacturer is not liable for profits lost by the brokerage firm because of the lack of computers unless, when the contract was entered into, the manufacturer was aware of the firm’s need for such computers to conduct business and the lost profits can be proven with reasonable certainty.

Consequential Damages—Contracts

Consequential damages are recoverable if they were the natural and probable consequences of the breach, if they were “in the contemplation of the parties at the time the contract was made,” or if they were otherwise foreseeable. Example: A computer manufacturer breaches a contract to deliver computers to a brokerage firm for use in the firm’s business. The computer manufacturer is not liable for profits lost by the brokerage firm because of the lack of computers unless, when the contract was entered into, the manufacturer was aware of the firm’s need for such computers to conduct business and the lost profits can be proven with reasonable certainty.

rules.rule_statement: Rule To recover damages, the plaintiff must establish damages with reasonable certainty. Lost profits or earnings are particularly susceptible to challenge for uncertainty, especially when those profits or earnings are claimed for a new business. Prospective (i.e., future) harm is also subject to attack on the grounds of uncertainty. When lost profits are considered too speculative, such as with a new venture, courts often limit a party’s recovery to reliance damages (i.e., reasonable expenditures made in connection with the contract).

rules.rule_statement: Rule Under the avoidable consequences rule, a plaintiff is denied recovery for any harm or loss that she could have reasonably avoided. The burden is on the defendant to show that the plaintiff could have avoided such damages. Although sometimes referred to as a “duty to mitigate damages,” the avoidable consequences rule does not give the defendant a right of action against the plaintiff when the plaintiff fails to mitigate damages but, as noted, merely limits the damages the plaintiff can recover.

rules.rule_statement: A benefit received by the plaintiff as a direct consequence of the defendant’s tortious conduct may offset the damages to which the plaintiff is entitled. The benefit must causally stem from the tort, relate to the same interest of the plaintiff that was injured, and be a benefit that the plaintiff wants. If a defendant’s breach results in savings to the plaintiff, then such savings must be subtracted from the plaintiff’s damages.

Direct Beneﬁt
A benefit received by the plaintiff as a direct consequence of the defendant’s tortious conduct may offset the damages to which the plaintiff is entitled. The benefit must causally stem from the tort, relate to the same interest of the plaintiff that was injured, and be a benefit that the plaintiff wants. If a defendant’s breach results in savings to the plaintiff, then such savings must be subtracted from the plaintiff’s damages.

rules.rule_statement: A benefit (e.g., payment of medical expenses, lost wages) received by the plaintiff from a third party (i.e., a collateral source, such as an insurer) as a result of the harm suffered by the plaintiff due to the defendant’s conduct does not reduce the damages to which the plaintiff is entitled from the defendant. Note, however, that almost half of the states have modified or abolished this collateral-source rule, particularly when the defendant is a healthcare provider or public entity. The collateral-source rule is not as frequently applied, nor is it as widely accepted, in contract law as it is in tort law. The rule is most often applied to unemployment benefits received by a plaintiff that are not treated as reducing recovery under an employment contract.

Note, however, that almost half of the states have modified or abolished this collateral-source rule, particularly when the defendant is a healthcare provider or public entity. The collateral-source rule is not as frequently applied, nor is it as widely accepted, in contract law as it is in tort law. rules.rule_statement: Emotional distress damages are generally not awarded in a breach-of-contract action.
rules.short_rule_statement: Emotional distress damages are generally not awarded in a breach-of-contract action.
rules.source_text:
Emotional Distress Damages—Contract
Emotional distress damages are generally not awarded in a breach-of-contract action.

rules.rule_statement: If the breach is particularly likely to cause emotional distress, then damages for such distress may be recovered. Traditionally, such damages are limited to contracts involving the improper transportation or disposition of a dead body, the erroneous transmission of a message about death, the denial or termination of service by a utility, or improper acts by a common carrier or innkeeper. If the breach also results in a tort, such as the intentional infliction of emotional distress, then damages for emotional distress can be recovered.

Exceptions

Traditionally, such damages are limited to contracts involving the improper transportation or disposition of a dead body, the erroneous transmission of a message about death, the denial or termination of service by a utility, or improper acts by a common carrier or innkeeper. If the breach also results in a tort, such as the intentional infliction of emotional distress, then damages for emotional distress can be recovered.

rules.rule_statement: Special damages, also known as “consequential damages,” must be specifically pleaded. Failure to do so can result in denial of recovery for such damages. Because special damages, unlike general damages, do not necessarily naturally flow from a legally recognized injury or harm, such as lost wages in a personal-injury action, the plaintiff is required to give notice of such damages to the defendant. R2d Torts §§ 430–432, 902; R2d Contracts § 347. R2d Torts §§ 435, 435A. Hadley v. Baxendale, 156 Eng. Rep. 145 (1854); R2d Contracts § 351; UCC § 2-715. R2d Torts § 912; R2d Contracts § 352. rules.rule_statement: In general, prejudgment interest is awarded when damages are either liquidated to a specific sum or ascertainable by a fixed standard (e.g., a specific number of goods at a set price). Interest may also be provided for in the contract, subject to restrictions such as usury laws, or by statute. Prejudgment interest is generally not recoverable on nonmonetary damages, such as pain and suffering, or punitive damages. Post- judgment interest is routinely awarded and often statutorily mandated. R2d Torts § 913; R2d Contracts § 354.

rules.rule_statement: Litigation expenses, including court costs and attorney’s fees, are generally recoverable only if authorized by statute or provided for by contract. Expenses of a prior litigation may be recovered as damages if the defendant’s tortious conduct caused the litigation (e.g., malicious prosecution). R2d Torts § 914.

rules.rule_statement: An injunction is a court order directing a person to act or to refrain from acting. An injunction is usually issued by a trial court with equity jurisdiction and enforceable through a contempt sanction. It may serve as the sole remedy for a party or operate as a stopgap measure to preserve the status quo until further judicial proceedings can take place.

Injunctions
An injunction is a court order directing a person to act or to refrain from acting. An injunction is usually issued by a trial court with equity jurisdiction and enforceable through a contempt sanction. It may serve as the sole remedy for a party or operate as a stopgap measure to preserve the status quo until further judicial proceedings can take place.

rules.rule_statement: A prejudgment injunction is often sought to prevent irreparable harm to the plaintiff until a court action is resolved. There are two main types of prejudgment injunctions: A temporary restraining order; and ii. A preliminary injunction.
rules.short_rule_statement: A prejudgment injunction is often sought to prevent irreparable harm to the plaintiff until a court action is resolved. There are two main types of prejudgment injunctions: A...
rules.source_text:
Prejudgment Injunctions
A prejudgment injunction is often sought to prevent irreparable harm to the plaintiff until a court action is resolved. There are two main types of prejudgment injunctions: A temporary restraining order; and ii. A preliminary injunction.

rules.rule_statement: A temporary restraining order (TRO) is an injunction for a short time. Under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, a TRO expires on a date set by the court not to exceed 14 days after entry of the order unless the court extends it for good cause or the adverse party consents. Under the California Civil Procedure Code, a TRO issued without notice to the defendant generally cannot exceed 15 days. A TRO may be obtained ex parte, that is, without notice to the defendant. A primary purpose of the TRO is to preserve the status quo until a preliminary injunction hearing can be held. A TRO cannot be appealed, but it is not effective until the defendant receives notice of it.
rules.short_rule_statement: A temporary restraining order (TRO) is an injunction for a short time. Under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, a TRO expires on a date set by the court not to exceed 14...
rules.source_text:
Temporary Restraining Order
A temporary restraining order (TRO) is an injunction for a short time. Under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, a TRO expires on a date set by the court not to exceed 14 days after entry of the order unless the court extends it for good cause or the adverse party consents. Under the California Civil Procedure Code, a TRO issued without notice to the defendant generally cannot exceed 15 days. A TRO may be obtained ex parte, that is, without notice to the defendant. A primary purpose of the TRO is to preserve the status quo until a preliminary injunction hearing can be held. A TRO cannot be appealed, but it is not effective until the defendant receives notice of it.

rules.rule_statement: A preliminary injunction is an injunction issued before or during a trial that generally is effective until the court issues a final judgment. Unlike the TRO, a preliminary injunction, which is also known as a temporary or interlocutory injunction, cannot be issued unless the defendant has been given notice of the hearing and an opportunity to be heard.
rules.short_rule_statement: A preliminary injunction is an injunction issued before or during a trial that generally is effective until the court issues a final judgment. Unlike the TRO, a preliminary...
rules.source_text:
Preliminary Injunction
A preliminary injunction is an injunction issued before or during a trial that generally is effective until the court issues a final judgment. Unlike the TRO, a preliminary injunction, which is also known as a temporary or interlocutory injunction, cannot be issued unless the defendant has been given notice of the hearing and an opportunity to be heard.

rules.rule_statement: Because an injunction is an equitable remedy, a court generally has discretion as to the issuance of the injunction and its form. Rule To obtain a prejudgment injunction, the plaintiff must usually establish that: The plaintiff will suffer irreparable harm if the injunction is not issued; ii. The plaintiff’s hardships, if the injunction is not issued, will be significantly greater than the hardships on the defendant and third parties if the injunction is issued (i.e., a balancing of the equities in the plaintiff’s favor); and iii. There is a likelihood that the plaintiff will succeed on the merits. In balancing the equities, generally the defendant and third parties should not suffer undue hardship as a consequence of the injunction. EXAM NOTE: You should thoroughly analyze any arguments that the plaintiff and defendant would make regarding hardship.  And keep in mind that a defendant can reference hardship to third parties in support of the defendant’s argument.

rules.canonical_name: Lack Of Adequate Remedy At Law
rules.rule_statement: To show that he will suffer irreparable harm if the injunction is not issued, the plaintiff is usually required to establish the lack of an adequate remedy at law. Example: If a plaintiff seeks an injunction to force a defendant to repair a damaged table, then a monetary payment by the defendant to the plaintiff may adequately compensate the plaintiff for the table’s loss in value. By contrast, if a plaintiff seeks an injunction to force a defendant to return a table, then a similar monetary payment based on the value of the table may not adequately compensate the plaintiff if the table itself, as a family heirloom, has a noneconomic value to him.

rules.rule_statement: A request for an injunction may be denied if it is not feasible for the court to enforce. In particular, courts have traditionally been reluctant to issue injunctions that require extensive involvement and continued supervision by the court. However, if the underlying rights are sufficiently important, such as constitutional rights, then the court may issue such injunctions.

Enforceability

In particular, courts have traditionally been reluctant to issue injunctions that require extensive involvement and continued supervision by the court. However, if the underlying rights are sufficiently important, such as constitutional rights, then the court may issue such injunctions.

rules.rule_statement: To issue an injunction that prohibits future acts, the plaintiff must show a real and immediate threat that such acts will occur. The fact that similar acts have occurred in the past does not necessarily justify an injunction against the future acts. If the plaintiff does establish the need for an injunction against future acts, then she may also be entitled to a remedy, such as damages, for past acts.

The fact that similar acts have occurred in the past does not necessarily justify an injunction against the future acts. If the plaintiff does establish the need for an injunction against future acts, then she may also be entitled to a remedy, such as damages, for past acts.

rules.rule_statement: For a prejudgment injunction, preservation of the status quo is usually a primary concern. Consequently, prohibitory injunctions (i.e., injunctions that restrain action) are generally favored over mandatory injunctions (i.e., injunctions that require action). However, some circumstances require mandatory injunctions to preserve the status quo. Example: An injunction that orders the reinstatement of a student on an athletic team pending a final determination of the student’s right to participate preserves the status quo for the student. R2d Torts § 936, cmt. d. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 527.

rules.rule_statement: A permanent injunction is issued to prevent the violation of the plaintiff’s rights or to restore to the plaintiff rights that have been violated.

Permanent Injunction
A permanent injunction is issued to prevent the violation of the plaintiff’s rights or to restore to the plaintiff rights that have been violated.

rules.rule_statement: Rule To obtain a permanent injunction, the plaintiff must usually establish that: She will suffer irreparable harm if the injunction is not issued; and ii. Her hardships, if the injunction is not issued, will be significantly greater than the hardships on the defendant and third parties if the injunction is issued. These are the same two factors that apply to a prejudgment injunction. However, unlike with a prejudgment injunction, with a permanent injunction, the plaintiff’s likelihood of success on the merits is not a factor, because she has already prevailed on the merits.

rules.rule_statement: As an equitable remedy, an injunction is subject to the equitable defenses of laches and unclean hands. In addition, an injunction is subject to constitutional restrictions, such as when the injunction would interfere with the defendant’s freedom of speech.

rules.source_text:
Defenses Against Injunction
As an equitable remedy, an injunction is subject to the equitable defenses of laches and unclean hands. In addition, an injunction is subject to constitutional restrictions, such as when the injunction would interfere with the defendant’s freedom of speech.

rules.rule_statement: Laches is the denial of equitable relief because of an unreasonable delay in pursuing an equitable remedy of which the plaintiff was aware or should have been aware (e.g., unavailability of a witness) and that caused prejudice to the defendant. The doctrine of laches is a failure to assert one’s rights in a timely manner, resulting in a claim being barred. The reasoning behind this doctrine is that the delay in asserting the claim may have caused a great increase in the potential damages to be awarded or that assets that could earlier have been used to satisfy the claim may have been distributed in the meantime. In other words, the defendant suffers a greater harm than if the claim had been timely asserted.

rules.rule_statement: Under the unclean hands doctrine, the plaintiff is not entitled to an equitable remedy because he is acting unethically or has acted in bad faith regarding the subject of the complaint. R2d Torts § 936, cmt. e. R2d Torts §§ 939, 940. R3d Restitution § 70. R3d Restitution § 70. R3d Restitution § 63.

rules.rule_statement: Specific performance is a frequently tested remedy on the exam. Specific performance is a contractual remedy by which a party to a contract is compelled by court order to render the promised performance or a substitute. Generally, it is not available unless there has been a breach of contract by nonperformance or repudiation. Rule For a court to grant a plaintiff’s request for specific performance, these requirements must be met: There must be a contract; ii. The contract terms must be sufficiently certain to provide a basis for the court order; iii. The conditions required for the defendant’s performance must be satisfied; iv. Damages must be inadequate to protect the plaintiff’s expectation interest; Relief must be equitable; and vi. Enforcement must be feasible.

rules.rule_statement: For any contract to be enforceable, certainty of the contract terms is a basic requirement. Without such certainty, a contract does not exist, and the plaintiff is not entitled to damages or specific performance, although restitution may be available as a remedy. Often when a term is missing or incomplete, the court can supply the missing term or complete the term by relying on past dealings of the parties, trade usage, or a gap-filler provision in the law.

rules.rule_statement: To compel specific performance by a defendant, any conditions precedent to that performance must have been satisfied or excused. In addition, if each party’s performance is a concurrent condition for the other party’s performance, then the plaintiff must have either performed the contractual obligations on which the defendant’s performance is conditioned or provided adequate security that he will do so.

rules.rule_statement: Damages are inadequate if the property to be transferred is unique (e.g., land, artwork, a rare manuscript). In addition, in the case of the buyer of goods, the buyer’s inability to cover (i.e., purchase substitute goods from another seller) gives rise to a right to specific performance, even if such goods are not unique.

rules.rule_statement: The adequacy of damages regarding part of the contract does not prevent specific performance from being a remedy for the contract as a whole. Example: When a contract for the sale of a business involved the transfer of goods as well as land, then the transfer of the goods in addition to the land could be ordered even though damages would have been an adequate remedy regarding the goods.

However, if the contract specifically provides for payment as an alternative to performance, then specific performance can be granted only on the condition that such payment is not made.

rules.rule_statement: A court will not specifically enforce a promise if enforcement would impose burdens on the court that are disproportionate to the advantages to be gained from its enforcement and the harm to be suffered from its denial. Performance that would entail long, continuous supervision (e.g., a construction contract) or that would involve judgment as to the suitability of the performance (e.g., a personal-services contract) will generally not be ordered. R2d Contracts § 359(3). R2d Contracts § 361. R2d Contracts § 364. R2d Contracts § 366.

rules.rule_statement: Reformation, an equitable restitutionary remedy, is a judicial rewriting of an agreement (e.g., a contract) or other document (e.g., a deed) to reflect the parties’ true intent. Reformation is often a stepping stone to another remedy, such as damages or restitution.

Reformation
Reformation, an equitable restitutionary remedy, is a judicial rewriting of an agreement (e.g., a contract) or other document (e.g., a deed) to reflect the parties’ true intent. Reformation is often a stepping stone to another remedy, such as damages or restitution.

rules.rule_statement: For a writing to be reformed, the parties must have entered into an agreement. If an agreement does not exist, such as when a valid offer and acceptance are lacking because a necessary term is missing, then reformation cannot be used to create such an agreement.

Agreement Required
For a writing to be reformed, the parties must have entered into an agreement. If an agreement does not exist, such as when a valid offer and acceptance are lacking because a necessary term is missing, then reformation cannot be used to create such an agreement.

rules.rule_statement: If a writing purports to reflect the parties’ prior agreement, then that writing may be reformed to conform to that prior agreement if both parties were unaware that it did not do so (i.e., there is a mutual mistake).

rules.source_text:
Reformation Based On Mutual Mistake
If a writing purports to reflect the parties’ prior agreement, then that writing may be reformed to conform to that prior agreement if both parties were unaware that it did not do so (i.e., there is a mutual mistake).

rules.rule_statement: The failure of the writing to reflect the parties’ agreement can result from the omission of an agreed-to term, the inclusion of a term not agreed to, or the alteration of an agreed-to term (e.g., “Lot 3” instead of “Lot 4”). Such mistakes typically are referred to as “scrivener’s errors.” In addition to stemming from the content of the writing, the failure of the writing to reflect the parties’ agreement can stem from the parties’ mistake as to the effect of the writing. Example: A contract that contains a clause intentionally inserted by the parties making one of the parties a surety of a debt, even though the parties thought that the clause had the effect of making that party only a guarantor of the debt, as was their agreement, can be reformed to make that party a guarantor of the debt.

Types Of Mistakes
The failure of the writing to reflect the parties’ agreement can result from the omission of an agreed-to term, the inclusion of a term not agreed to, or the alteration of an agreed-to term (e.g., “Lot 3” instead of “Lot 4”). Such mistakes typically are referred to as “scrivener’s errors.” In addition to stemming from the content of the writing, the failure of the writing to reflect the parties’ agreement can stem from the parties’ mistake as to the effect of the writing. Example: A contract that contains a clause intentionally inserted by the parties making one of the parties a surety of a debt, even though the parties thought that the clause had the effect of making that party only a guarantor of the debt, as was their agreement, can be reformed to make that party a guarantor of the debt.

rules.rule_statement: There must have been an agreement between the parties before the writing. The agreement need not have been sufficient to create a contract, but it must relate to the mistake in the writing. The agreement must be sufficiently certain to permit the court to restate the writing in accordance with the agreement.

Unlike the avoidance of a contract due to mutual mistake, a party is not required to show that the mistake has resulted in an inequality that adversely affects him.

rules.rule_statement: When only one party is mistaken as to the content or effect of the writing, reformation turns on whether the other party is aware of the mistake. If the other party is aware of the mistake and either has fraudulently induced the mistaken party’s erroneous belief or does nothing to correct the mistaken party’s erroneous belief, then the mistaken party may seek reformation of the contract. If the other party is unaware of the mistake, then reformation is not allowed.

If the other party is aware of the mistake and either has fraudulently induced the mistaken party’s erroneous belief or does nothing to correct the mistaken party’s erroneous belief, then the mistaken party may seek reformation of the contract. If the other party is unaware of the mistake, then reformation is not allowed.

rules.rule_statement: In addition to the general equitable defenses of laches and unclean hands, reformation is subject to the rights of innocent third parties. A good-faith purchaser for value may prevent the reformation of a writing, such as to impose a mortgage on the acquired property. Alternatively, a judgment creditor or trustee in bankruptcy who acquires property is not protected from reformation of a writing that affects his rights in the property.

rules.rule_statement: Equitable defenses, as the name implies, are defenses to equitable actions. As such, they are remedial defenses rather than substantive defenses. With equitable defenses, the court has the discretion to refuse to apply them if there are countervailing equitable considerations. Moreover, these defenses do not prevent a plaintiff from bringing an action at law for damages.

rules.rule_statement: Laches is the denial of equitable relief because of an unreasonable delay in pursuing an equitable remedy of which the plaintiff was aware or should have been aware (e.g., unavailability of a witness), and which delay caused prejudice to the defendant. R3d Restitution § 70. In general, laches operates with respect to equitable actions in a similar manner as the statute of limitations does to legal actions. When there is an analogous statute of limitations and its period for bringing suit has expired, the burden of establishing the reasonableness of the delay is placed on the plaintiff; when it has not expired, the burden of establishing that the delay was unreasonable is placed on the defendant.

rules.rule_statement: The defendant’s retention of the benefit must be unjust. Unjust (i.e., unjustified) enrichment is enrichment that lacks an adequate legal basis; it results from a transaction that the law treats as ineffective to work a conclusive alteration in ownership rights. The mere fact that a defendant benefits from the plaintiff’s action is not sufficient to require restitution of the benefit. Example: A landowner whose property value increases because of improvements made by a neighbor on the neighbor’s own property has not been unjustly enriched. Often, the defendant’s retention of the benefit is unjust because the defendant has wrongfully acquired the benefit from the plaintiff, such as through theft, fraud, duress, or undue influence. Even a defendant who acquires a benefit without fault, breach of duty, or mistake, such as an unconscious accident victim who receives medical care, may be unjustly enriched.

rules.rule_statement: A conscious wrongdoer is a person who acts with knowledge of the underlying wrong or despite a known risk that the conduct violates the claimant’s rights. The enrichment of a conscious wrongdoer is the greater of the net profit attained by the wrongdoing or the market value of the benefits wrongfully obtained. This type of remedy is typically known as disgorgement, accounting, or accounting for profits. The burden of producing a reasonable approximation of the amount of wrongful gain is on the plaintiff seeking disgorgement of profit. A fiduciary who fails to adhere to his fiduciary obligations (i.e., a defaulting fiduciary) is usually treated as a conscious wrongdoer, even though the fiduciary did not have notice of the failure or was not at fault for his failure.

A fiduciary who fails to adhere to his fiduciary obligations (i.e., a defaulting fiduciary) is usually treated as a conscious wrongdoer, even though the fiduciary did not have notice of the failure or was not at fault for his failure.

rules.rule_statement: A constructive trust is a judicially created remedy that imposes a duty to convey property to the plaintiff if the defendant would be unjustly enriched by retaining it. Instead of providing the plaintiff with monetary restitution, a constructive trust gives the plaintiff restitution of specific property. Example: If a corporate director has usurped an opportunity belonging to the corporation, then the corporation can seek a constructive trust regarding the opportunity (e.g., land, building). Similarly, a constructive trust is often the remedy sought for property acquired by fraud. A plaintiff seeking a constructive trust must show that: ii) The defendant’s retention of the property would unjustly enrich the defendant; and iii) The legal remedy is inadequate. In such cases, the defendant may be declared a constructive trustee, for the plaintiff’s benefit, of the property in question and its traceable product. EXAM NOTE: To show that the defendant’s retention of the property would be unjust, the plaintiff must show that the defendant committed some tort or breach of duty (e.g., embezzlement, conversion, fraud). A constructive trust may not be sought against someone who has possession of but not title to the property in question (such as a thief). A constructive trust may, however, be sought against a third-party transferee who has title to the property, unless that person is a bona fide purchaser. The effect of a constructive trust is that the plaintiff acquires the property itself. The plaintiff is not required to account to the defendant for any increase in the property’s value during the defendant’s ownership of the property; similarly, the defendant is not liable to the plaintiff for any decrease in the property’s value during that time. EXAM NOTE: Recent essay questions testing these concepts involved tort claims of conversion (misappropriation of money) and fraud (ﬁnancial harm).

rules.rule_statement: An equitable lien is a judicially created remedy that recognizes the plaintiff’s claim against the defendant by imposing a lien on the defendant’s property as security for that claim. As is the case with a constructive trust, the defendant must be unjustly enriched, and that enrichment must relate to the property to which a lien attaches. The requirements for an equitable lien are essentially the same as those for a constructive trust except that the plaintiff who provides services that improve the defendant’s property may be entitled to an equitable lien on such property, even though the plaintiff would not be entitled to a constructive trust regarding such property. A plaintiff may have a choice between an equitable lien and a constructive trust. Example: If the defendant embezzles funds from the plaintiff and uses the funds to buy a house, then the plaintiff can seek an equitable lien or a constructive trust with respect to the house. However, if the defendant owned the house before embezzling the funds and instead used the funds to add a room to the house, then the plaintiff can only pursue an equitable lien on the house as a remedy. Because unlike a constructive trust, an equitable lien does not exchange the plaintiff’s claim for the defendant’s property, an equitable lien is usually more advantageous if the property’s value has declined. This is because the plaintiff usually can continue to seek satisfaction of the remaining portion of the claim from the defendant personally after the property has been sold and the proceeds given to the plaintiff. Conversely, when the property’s value has risen, the constructive trust is a better option for the plaintiff because the plaintiff is not required to account to the defendant for the amount by which the property’s value exceeds the amount of the claim. EXAM NOTE: A plaintiff should pursue a constructive trust if his property value has increased but pursue an equitable lien if his property value has decreased.

rules.rule_statement: Subrogation is a judicially created remedy that permits the plaintiff to stand in the shoes of a creditor or lienholder if the plaintiff’s property was used to discharge an obligation or lien on the defendant’s property. As with a constructive trust and an equitable lien, the defendant must be unjustly enriched by this discharge. However, unlike in a constructive trust and an equitable lien, the enrichment does not take the form of property but instead of the discharge of an obligation or lien. Subrogation is also known as an “equitable assignment,” with the assignment being made by the court, not the parties. A common situation in which subrogation arises is a surety paying another’s debt. Subrogation is also applicable when the defendant fraudulently obtains money from the plaintiff and uses it to satisfy the defendant’s debt. Under subrogation, the plaintiff enjoys any rights and remedies possessed by the creditor or lienholder against the defendant, which frequently gives the plaintiff priority over the defendant’s other creditors. However, the plaintiff is also subject to any defenses that the defendant has against the creditor or lienholder. The plaintiff’s recovery is limited to reimbursement of money or other property paid by the plaintiff to discharge the defendant’s obligation.

rules.rule_statement: Awarding restitution to the plaintiff may be inequitable when an innocent defendant has changed position in reliance on the benefit. This defense is not available if the defendant is a conscious wrongdoer. In the following situations, the defendant’s change in position may prevent the plaintiff from being entitled to restitution: faith passes such property onto the principal or beneficiary; ii) The defendant mistakenly receives a benefit, which the defendant expends or consumes (e.g., takes a trip), and the expenditure or consumption would not have been undertaken if not for the mistake; or iii) The benefit has been lost, stolen, or destroyed and cannot be returned. In Situation (iii), if the benefit is merely damaged or diminished, then restitution may be reduced under the diminished- value rule, rather than denied completely. Generally, Situation (iii) applies when the defendant received the benefit by mistake, but it does not apply when the defendant requested the benefit, as in a contractual relationship.

rules.rule_statement: Equitable restitution is not available against a bona fide purchaser who takes without notice of the prior equities. Example: A buyer who acquires property from a seller by fraud generally can transfer legal title to the property to a subsequent buyer. If the subsequent buyer does not have notice of the original buyer’s fraud, then the subsequent buyer’s ownership of the property is not subject to challenge by the defrauded seller. This protection is not available to the current holder of the property who has not acquired the property by purchase (e.g., a donee).

components.source_text: 1)      Protection of another’s life or health

rules.rule_statement: Generally, a plaintiff who unconditionally confers a benefit on a defendant is not entitled to restitution if the benefit was conferred without mistake, coercion, or request. In other words, a volunteer or an intermeddler generally cannot seek restitution for an unsolicited benefit provided to a defendant. However, if the circumstances justify the decision to intervene without request, certain exceptions may apply. A person who provides professional services required to protect another’s life or health is entitled to restitution from the other as necessary to prevent unjust enrichment. Unjust enrichment is measured by a reasonable charge for the services in question. A person who takes effective action to protect another’s property from threatened harm is entitled to restitution from the other as necessary to prevent unjust enrichment. Unrequested intervention is justified only when it is reasonable to assume the owner would wish the action performed. Unjust enrichment is measured by the loss avoided or by a reasonable charge for the services provided, whichever is less. A person who performs another’s duty to a third person or to the public is entitled to restitution from the other as necessary to prevent unjust enrichment. Unrequested intervention may be justified in: i) Paying another’s money debt if there is no prejudice to the obligor in substituting a liability in restitution for the original obligation; ii) Performing another’s duty to furnish necessaries to a third person, to avoid imminent harm to the interests of the third person; or iii) Performing another’s duty to the public if performance is urgently required to protect public health, safety, or general welfare. There is no unjust enrichment and no claim in restitution except insofar as the claimant’s intervention has relieved the defendant of an otherwise enforceable obligation. rules.rule_statement: Rule A plaintiff may bring a common-law ejectment action to regain possession of land held by another. The plaintiff generally must establish title to the land and the right to immediate possession of it. Because this action is in rem, not in personam, the judgment is directed at the sheriff, who is ordered to take possession of the property and return it to the plaintiff.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff may also seek damages as part of an ejectment action. The general measure of damages is the value of the use of the land (i.e., its rental value). These damages may include profits related to the defendant’s use of the land (e.g., rents collected by the defendant from tenants), which are referred to as “mesne profits.” In addition, the plaintiff is entitled to damages for any harm caused to the land during the defendant’s possession.

rules.rule_statement: The traditional rule is that a defendant who has ousted the plaintiff is not entitled to compensation for improvements made to the plaintiff’s property. However, the defendant who acted in good faith in possessing the plaintiff’s property may be entitled to set off the value of such improvements when the plaintiff seeks damages as well as possession. In addition, a betterment statute may permit the good-faith defendant to remove the improvement or, in the alternative, allow the landowner to pay for the improvement.

rules.rule_statement: A mere trespass is a defendant’s trespass on the plaintiff’s land that otherwise does not harm the land, such as when the defendant walks across the plaintiff’s land. The plaintiff is entitled to nominal damages when the defendant commits mere trespass. The plaintiff may also be entitled to consequential damages (if the trespass harms the plaintiff’s business) or even punitive damages (if the defendant trespasses after the plaintiff has refused the defendant permission to enter the land). However, damages are not a sufficient remedy because the plaintiff would receive only nominal damages and, if repeated trespasses were anticipated, multiple lawsuits would be necessary. A plaintiff may seek an injunction to prevent a defendant’s future trespass. Traditionally, a plaintiff was generally not allowed restitution for a defendant’s trespass on the plaintiff’s land. The modern trend, however, is to allow a monetary recovery. If the defendant is a conscious wrongdoer, then the measure of recovery is the disgorgement of all gains, including consequential gains derived by the defendant from the trespass; if the defendant is merely an innocent or negligent trespasser, then recovery is typically measured by the reasonable rental value or reasonable cost of a license.

rules.rule_statement: An encroachment is a defendant’s trespassory use and possession of the plaintiff’s land by virtue of a structure (e.g., building) or other object (e.g., tree). The plaintiff is entitled to damages stemming from the encroachment. When the encroachment is permanent, damages are measured by the reduction in value of the plaintiff’s land. If the encroachment is temporary, damages include the cost to remove the encroachment and restore the land to its pre-encroachment condition and the value of the loss of use suffered by the plaintiff during the encroachment (e.g., the rental value of the land subject to the encroachment). A plaintiff may be entitled to a mandatory injunction ordering the defendant to remove the encroachment. The legal remedy of damages is inadequate because land is unique.

Intentional Encroachment
If the defendant has notice that the structure will encroach upon the plaintiff’s property (i.e., intentional encroachment), then the plaintiff is entitled to a mandatory injunction ordering removal of the structure.

rules.rule_statement: If the defendant does not have notice that a structure will encroach upon the plaintiff’s property (i.e., innocent encroachment), then the plaintiff is entitled to a mandatory injunction unless, after weighing the equities, the court concludes that the defendant’s hardship in removing the structure is significantly greater than the injury to the plaintiff from the denial of the injunction (i.e., the disproportionate burden test, which is a form of the general balancing of the hardships requirement for injunctions). Note: When the plaintiff is denied an injunction, the plaintiff is usually entitled to damages. Particularly in the case of plants, a plaintiff may be entitled to self-help to remove the encroachment (e.g., saw off the overhanging limb of the neighbor’s tree) without resort to legal process.

A plaintiff is entitled to damages when the defendant trespasses to sever things attached to the plaintiff’s land. A plaintiff is entitled to damages when the defendant trespasses to sever things attached to the plaintiff’s land. rules.rule_statement: Alternatively, damages may be based on the severed items’ value as goods; in this case, the action effectively becomes one for conversion rather than trespass to land. If the defendant’s trespass was innocent (e.g., the defendant believed that the trees were on his land), then the damages may account for the value that the defendant’s efforts added to the goods (e.g., timber valued in place without increase in value that reflects the cost of cutting and transporting timber). This typically equates with the value of the right to sever such items (e.g., the value of the right to mine a known mineral deposit).

rules.rule_statement: A defendant’s liability to the plaintiff may stem from the defendant’s trespass on the plaintiff’s land to place items on the land, such as dumping construction debris on the land or storing natural gas in an underground formation that extends into the plaintiff’s property. A plaintiff is entitled to damages if the defendant trespasses to place things on or in the plaintiff’s land. The general measure of damages is either the value of the use of the land that was lost (i.e., its rental value) or the cost of restoring the land to its pre-trespass condition (e.g., the cost of removing the debris). However, the legal remedy of damages is not sufficient because of the unique character of land. A plaintiff may seek a mandatory injunction to require the defendant to remove the things placed on or in the plaintiff’s land. A plaintiff is also entitled to restitution if the defendant trespasses to place things on or in the plaintiff’s land. The plaintiff can seek a money judgment remedy to the same extent as allowed under the modern trend for a mere trespass.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant’s liability to the plaintiff may stem from the defendant’s interference with the plaintiff’s use of an easement, including actions that result in the destruction of or injury to the easement. The plaintiff is entitled to damages for the interference with his easement. When the easement has been destroyed or injured, the measure of damages is usually the reduction in value of the easement. When the interference does not destroy or injure the easement, the measure of damages is usually the value of the loss of use of the easement during the period of the interference. The plaintiff may also seek nominal damages to acknowledge his easement and to prevent its loss. In addition, punitive damages may be available if the defendant’s actions in interfering with the easement are willful or malicious. The plaintiff may seek an injunction when the defendant interferes with the plaintiff’s easement rights. Generally, legal remedies will be inadequate because of the uniqueness of the plaintiff’s specific property right and, when the interference is ongoing, the need for multiple damage actions. The injunction can be either mandatory, such as ordering the removal of a fence across an easement, or prohibitory, such as preventing the use of more parking spaces than permitted under a lease. An injunction is not granted when, after weighing the equities, the court concludes that the defendant has satisfied the disproportionate hardship test; in such a case, the plaintiff is instead entitled to seek damages. The plaintiff may enter the defendant’s land (i.e., the servient estate) to remove anything that interferes with the plaintiff’s use of the easement on the defendant’s land. The plaintiff’s entry must not result in a breach of the peace. When the obstruction, or at least its interference with the plaintiff’s easement, is unknown to the defendant, the plaintiff must first request that the defendant remove it. The plaintiff is liable for any unnecessary damage to the defendant’s land. Because an easement is not a possessory interest in land, the plaintiff cannot bring an ejectment action for interference with an easement.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant’s liability to the plaintiff may stem from the defendant’s waste regarding property in which the plaintiff holds an interest. Waste is the transformation of property by a person rightfully in possession of that property that detrimentally affects another person’s interest in the same property (e.g., a landlord, the holder of a future interest in the property, a joint owner). A plaintiff is entitled to damages for waste. Whether the waste results from the defendant’s action (i.e., affirmative or voluntary waste) or inaction (i.e., permissive waste), the measure of damages is either the reduction in property value due to the waste or the repair cost. A number of states provide by statute for increased damage awards (e.g., treble damages) and forfeiture, particularly if the defendant’s actions were willful. The plaintiff is entitled to an injunction to prevent affirmative waste but generally not for permissive waste. When changes made by the plaintiff improve the property (i.e., ameliorative waste), the plaintiff is not entitled to damages but may be able to secure an injunction if the defendant’s property interest is of a short duration.

rules.rule_statement: For an individual plaintiff to maintain a damage action for a public nuisance, the plaintiff must suffer damage that is different in kind from that suffered by the general public. Generally, a plaintiff who suffers physical harm to himself or his real or personal property because of a public nuisance can recover damages. Furthermore, a plaintiff who suffers pecuniary loss that is different in kind from that suffered by the general public (e.g., a commercial fisherman who cannot fish in an area because of the defendant’s pollution) can recover damages. For a public-entity plaintiff, recovery of damages may, of course, be had when authorized by statute, but there is a conflict as to whether such damages may be recovered in the absence of statutory authority.

rules.rule_statement: In a private nuisance action, a plaintiff generally cannot be denied an injunction on the grounds that damages are an inadequate remedy; the harm suffered by the plaintiff usually relates to the plaintiff’s personal enjoyment of property. However, in balancing the equities, a court may nevertheless refuse to issue an injunction and leave the plaintiff with a damage remedy, even when the nuisance is intentional, as most nuisances are. Note: In determining whether a private nuisance exists, an initial balancing of the equities is required to ascertain if the defendant’s interference is unreasonable.

rules.rule_statement: If the loss of possession constitutes a conversion, then the date for measuring the value of the seized property is generally the date of the conversion. When the personal property fluctuates in value, the plaintiff may measure the value by the highest value in the period between the date that the plaintiff learned of the conversion and a reasonable time for securing a replacement. When the defendant refuses to return the property after a demand by the plaintiff, the date of the demand can also serve as the valuation date. When the defendant sells the property, the date of sale can also serve as the valuation date. If the defendant has improved the plaintiff’s personal property, then the plaintiff is entitled to recover the full value of the property if the defendant’s conduct was not in good faith. If the defendant’s conduct was innocent, then the defendant is entitled to a credit for the value of the improvement. If the plaintiff’s property has been successively converted by different defendants, then the time for determining the amount of each defendant’s liability is based on the time and place of that defendant’s conversion. If the plaintiff’s property has been converted, then the plaintiff is not required to accept an offer by the defendant to return the property, but he can treat the conversion as a forced sale of the property by the plaintiff to the defendant.

rules.rule_statement: When the plaintiff’s personal property is taken by the defendant, the plaintiff may pursue a replevin action. Through a replevin action, which is an action at law, the plaintiff can both regain possession of the property and recover damages for the loss of use of the property (i.e., detention damages). Before trial, the plaintiff may be able to obtain possession of the property through a writ of replevin, which requires the sheriff to seize the property and return it to the plaintiff. To obtain possession of the property this way, the plaintiff is required to post a bond; the defendant may be able to secure return of the property by posting a redelivery bond. Constitutional due-process concerns mandate a judge’s involvement in this process. When the plaintiff’s personal property is taken by the defendant, the plaintiff may seek a constructive trust or an equitable lien. These remedies are primarily used when the defendant is a fiduciary who converts the plaintiff’s property or when the defendant obtains the plaintiff’s property through fraud, duress, or undue influence. When the plaintiff’s personal property is taken by the defendant, the plaintiff may recover the unjust benefit received by the defendant through a restitutionary money judgment action. When the defendant has retained the property, the plaintiff’s action may be based on the fiction of goods sold and delivered; when the defendant has sold the property, the plaintiff’s action may be based on the fiction of money had and received.

rules.rule_statement: The measure of the plaintiff’s recovery is typically the market value of the property when the defendant acquired, retained, or disposed of the property or, if the property fluctuates in value, its highest value within a reasonable time after the tortious conduct. If the defendant has made improvements to the property, then the plaintiff is entitled to the value of the property, including the improvements, unless the defendant’s conversion of the property was innocent (i.e., in good faith and without knowledge that he was not entitled to the property). If the defendant has returned the property to the plaintiff, then the plaintiff is entitled to a loss-of-use recovery (i.e., the rental value of the property). If the plaintiff’s personal property is taken by the defendant and a legal remedy is inadequate, then the plaintiff may seek an injunction. Generally, to establish that the legal remedy is inadequate, the plaintiff must establish that the property is unique, which may include property that has a special value to the plaintiff (e.g., a wedding ring) as well as property that is one of a kind. Damages are an inadequate remedy for such property. A replevin action is generally an inadequate remedy because the defendant could thwart the sheriff’s seizure of the property by hiding it.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff can pursue a restitutionary money judgment action against a defendant who misappropriates the plaintiff’s money or other financial assets. The measure of recovery depends on the blameworthiness of the defendant’s conduct. A conscious wrongdoer or a person who acts despite a known risk that the conduct in question violates the plaintiff’s rights is required to disgorge all gains, including consequential gains, derived from the wrongful transaction. An innocent or negligent recipient is liable only for the direct benefit derived from the transaction, which can include interest income.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff can seek an equitable lien placed on funds appropriated by the defendant and used to acquire other property or to improve property owned by the defendant before the misappropriation. Note: A plaintiff is not entitled to a constructive trust on the improved property if the property was not acquired with the plaintiff’s funds. When the defendant commingles the plaintiff’s misappropriated funds with the defendant’s own funds, the plaintiff can seek an equitable lien on the commingled funds. Alternatively, the plaintiff can seek a constructive trust on the proportionate amount of the commingled fund that equals the plaintiff’s misappropriated funds.

rules.canonical_name: Physical And Emotional Consequences (Pain And Suffering)
rules.rule_statement: Pain and suffering damages are those that a plaintiff is entitled to for the impairment or loss of physical abilities such as movement or sight, other bodily harm, illness, and emotional distress caused by fear, anxiety, humiliation, or loss of freedom. The plaintiff need not suffer a pecuniary loss to be entitled to damages for these consequences; hence, they are frequently referred to as “noneconomic damages.” A plaintiff must be able to experience pain to recover for it. However, even a plaintiff who is in a vegetative state may be aware of pain (e.g., a comatose patient who responds to a pinprick by moaning) despite not being conscious in a more general sense. While an award of medical expenses is generally accompanied by a pain-and-suffering award, the two types of damages are not necessarily linked. An award of medical expenses does not automatically mandate a pain-and-suffering award. A plaintiff may recover for the loss of enjoyment of life (i.e., hedonic loss). However, about half of the states require that this loss be treated within the scope of pain-and-suffering damages, while the other half permit separate consideration of this loss. Damages for emotional distress are always allowed as ancillary damages when the plaintiff suffers physical injury that gives rise to a cause of action (e.g., assault, battery) or when the defendant’s action is intentional or outrageous. Limits may, however, be imposed on the recovery of damages solely for emotional distress arising from the defendant’s negligent conduct.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff is also entitled to recover any reasonable expenses necessarily incurred in treating an injury or illness suffered as a consequence of the defendant’s tortious conduct. Medical expenses for services rendered by healthcare providers (e.g., physician, nurse, dentist, hospital, and nursing home), drugs, and medical devices (e.g., artificial leg, wheelchair) are recoverable. In addition, other reasonable expenses resulting from the injury, such as additional housekeeping costs or travel expenses to obtain medical care, are allowed. Diagnostic expenses are allowed if they are reasonable in ascertaining injury or averting harm. Moreover, such expenses may be allowed if the defendant’s actions placed the plaintiff at risk for future injury without causing a present physical injury, such as exposing the plaintiff to toxic chemicals. Example: An X-ray taken of the plaintiff’s arm, which was tortiously struck by the defendant, is a reasonable expense, even if the X-ray reveals that the arm is not broken. The cost of future medical expenses is recoverable if the expenses are reasonably certain to be incurred in the future. The plaintiff is entitled to recover the reasonable cost of the medical goods and services. Bills received by the plaintiff from medical care providers are not necessarily conclusive proof of the reasonableness of the charges. Moreover, because reasonableness is the touchstone, the plaintiff may be entitled to recover even though the plaintiff has not paid such bills or has not been billed for such goods and services, such as when services are rendered without charge.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff who suffers a tortious physical injury can recover for loss or impairment of earning capacity. Recovery can include both the pretrial period from the time of the tort to the trial and any subsequent period during which the plaintiff suffers loss or impairment. While these damages are often referred to as damages for lost wages or earnings, the focus in determining the amount of damages is on the loss or diminution of the plaintiff’s earning capacity. For an employed plaintiff, the impact of the tort on the plaintiff’s actual earnings may be the best evidence of that diminution; however, a plaintiff who is unemployed at the time of the tort, including a child, student, or homemaker, can nevertheless be entitled to recover. Even a plaintiff who does not want to work can recover for loss of the opportunity to work.

rules.source_text:
Lost Earnings Capacity
A plaintiff who suffers a tortious physical injury can recover for loss or impairment of earning capacity. Recovery can include both the pretrial period from the time of the tort to the trial and any subsequent period during which the plaintiff suffers loss or impairment. While these damages are often referred to as damages for lost wages or earnings, the focus in determining the amount of damages is on the loss or diminution of the plaintiff’s earning capacity. For an employed plaintiff, the impact of the tort on the plaintiff’s actual earnings may be the best evidence of that diminution; however, a plaintiff who is unemployed at the time of the tort, including a child, student, or homemaker, can nevertheless be entitled to recover. Even a plaintiff who does not want to work can recover for loss of the opportunity to work.

rules.rule_statement: For a self-employed person or other individual who receives compensation for services rendered not in the form of wages or a salary but as measured by the profits of a business (e.g., a partner in a partnership), the measurement of his earning capacity is generally linked to the value of his services to the business. When such services are a critical component of the business’s profits, such profits can serve as a basis for measuring the value of his services. Otherwise, the market value of those services is used. Various factors are considered to determine a plaintiff’s future earnings capacity, including the plaintiff’s age, education, employment history, and physical condition.

Self-Employed Persons

When such services are a critical component of the business’s profits, such profits can serve as a basis for measuring the value of his services. Otherwise, the market value of those services is used. Various factors are considered to determine a plaintiff’s future earnings capacity, including the plaintiff’s age, education, employment history, and physical condition.

rules.rule_statement: If a plaintiff suffers a permanent injury or death, the reduction in the plaintiff’s life expectancy is not treated as a separate element of damages. Instead, the plaintiff’s life expectancy or working life expectancy as of the time of the tort is relevant in determining the plaintiff’s earnings capacity. Mortality tables based on the plaintiff’s age may be used to determine the relevant expectancy. The relevant expectancy derived from these tables may be adjusted to reflect the plaintiff’s deviation from the average physical condition or the dangerousness of the plaintiff’s occupation. In addition, the plaintiff’s physical condition at the time of the tort can account for any preexisting disease, illness, or other condition that is subsequently revealed, even though the plaintiff was unaware of that disease, illness, or condition until after the defendant’s tortious conduct.

rules.rule_statement: An award to compensate the plaintiff for the loss or diminution of future earnings capacity must be reduced to its present value to avoid overcompensating the plaintiff. This reduction accounts for the plaintiff’s ability to invest the award and earn interest on the invested funds. The modern trend is also to increase the amount of the award based on anticipated future inflation, which offsets the present value reduction.

An award to compensate the plaintiff for the loss or diminution of future earnings capacity must be reduced to its present value to avoid overcompensating the plaintiff. This reduction accounts for the plaintiff’s ability to invest the award and earn interest on the invested funds. The modern trend is also to increase the amount of the award based on anticipated future inflation, which offsets the present value reduction.

rules.rule_statement: While the plaintiff usually suffers a loss from the defendant’s tortious conduct, the defendant generally does not receive a tangible benefit as a consequence of that conduct that the defendant can be forced to return. There are limited circumstances, however, under which a defendant is denied benefits to which he would otherwise be entitled. For example, a defendant who committed murder to collect as a beneficiary under the victim’s insurance policy would forfeit those benefits.

rules.canonical_name: Defamation Relates To A Matter Of Public Concern
rules.rule_statement: If the plaintiff is a private person but the defamation relates to a matter of public concern, then the plaintiff must establish that the defendant was at fault in making the statement. The degree of fault determines what damages may be available. If the plaintiff establishes the defendant’s fault without proving actual malice, presumed and punitive damages may not be awarded. If the plaintiff is a private figure and the matter is not of public concern, presumed and punitive damages may be awarded even if the plaintiff establishes the defendant’s fault without proving actual malice. When the Constitution does not apply to a defamation action (i.e., when the defendant is a private person and the subject matter of the statement is not of public concern), a plaintiff can recover presumed damages without proof of actual injury in all libel actions and in slander per se actions, which include imputing a serious crime to the plaintiff or disparaging the plaintiff in the conduct of his business, trade, or profession (e.g., asserting a lawyer’s incompetency in handling a case). Note: Spoken defamation, unless recorded, is slander; written or recorded oral defamation is libel. A media defendant (e.g., a newspaper, a television station) may mitigate damages by publishing a retraction. Usually, only pecuniary (i.e., special) or actual damages can be recovered if a retraction has been published.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff who is being defamed by the defendant generally cannot seek an injunction for personal defamation. When the defamation relates to the plaintiff’s business activities (e.g., false advertising about a product produced by the plaintiff’s business), then the plaintiff may be able to seek an injunction. However, an injunction that is a prior restraint on speech is subject to challenge as an unconstitutional violation of the defendant’s free speech rights.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff whose privacy interests have been invaded by the defendant may seek an injunction. However, when the defendant’s actions involve publication of information about the plaintiff, an injunction may not be issued if the injunction would be an unconstitutional infringement on the defendant’s free speech rights. rules.rule_statement: Fraud (i.e., intentional misrepresentation) and negligent misrepresentation are considered economic tort actions rather than types of harm. As tort actions, each seeks to address financial harms, not property or personal harms, suffered in a business transaction. Fraud and negligent misrepresentation actions are usually limited in practice to transactions involving land, stocks, or intangibles or to the rendering of services, including employment benefits. For a sale of goods, a warranty action generally provides an easier path to recovery. Note: Intentional and negligent misrepresentation may constitute conduct for which other torts, such as battery, conversion, or negligence, provide an avenue by which property or personal harm can be recovered.

rules.rule_statement: When the defendant is liable for fraud, there are two ways in which the damages are measured: (1) loss of bargain and (2) out of pocket. Most jurisdictions recognize the loss-of-bargain measure, while some jurisdictions rely solely on the out-of- pocket measure. A few jurisdictions allow the plaintiff to elect the measure to be applied to determine damages.

rules.rule_statement: Under the loss-of-bargain measure, which is contract based, the buyer is entitled to the difference between the value of what she was promised and the market value of what she received. This measure of loss is grounded in the buyer’s expectancy interest. Instead of basing the loss-of-bargain measure on the value of what the buyer received, the buyer can base her recovery on an amount equal to the cost of conforming the property to what she was promised.

rules.rule_statement: When the defendant retains personal property belonging to the plaintiff that was obtained through misrepresentation, the plaintiff can pursue a replevin action for its return. When a seller of goods discovers that the buyer is insolvent after the buyer receives the goods, the seller has the right to reclaim the goods within a reasonable time after the buyer receives them. Having reclaimed the goods, the seller has no other remedy involving them, even though the buyer misrepresented his solvency.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff who has suffered financial harm because of the defendant’s interference with a contract between the plaintiff and a third party can recover damages. Such damages include not only the general contract damages that the plaintiff could otherwise seek from the other party to the contract (e.g., lost profits) but also special damages that are proximately caused by the defendant’s interference, including emotional distress damages. Particularly for an interference with a prospective economic advantage action, damages may be denied because of a lack of reasonable certainty as to their existence or amount. Punitive damages may be available if the defendant’s actions are willful or malicious. The plaintiff is entitled to maintain a breach-of-contract action against the other party to the contract in addition to a tort action against the defendant. However, although the other party to the contract and the defendant are not joint tortfeasors, a recovery obtained from one should reduce the other’s liability to the extent that there is duplication of damage payments.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, when the seller breaches a real property contract, the buyer is entitled to recover the difference between the contract price and the market value (i.e., loss-of-bargain damages). The measurement date is the date of the breach. The buyer may also be entitled to consequential and incidental damages as well as the return of any payments made on the purchase price. If the seller’s breach results from a title defect that the seller was unaware of and cannot correct (i.e., it is a good-faith breach), then some states limit the buyer’s recovery to out-of-pocket damages, such as the cost of a title search, and a return of any prepayments of the purchase price.

rules.rule_statement: For the buyer, land is viewed as a unique asset; consequently, monetary damages are inadequate. For the seller, the inability to escape the burden of ownership (e.g., taxes, maintenance responsibilities) is often the reason that monetary damages are inadequate. However, many courts also cite the fairness of treating the seller the same as the buyer, an affirmative twist to the traditional mutuality of remedy restriction, as a justification.
