Inadequacy Of Legal Remedy
For the buyer, land is viewed as a unique asset; consequently, monetary damages are inadequate. For the seller, the inability to escape the burden of ownership (e.g., taxes, maintenance responsibilities) is often the reason that monetary damages are inadequate. However, many courts also cite the fairness of treating the seller the same as the buyer, an affirmative twist to the traditional mutuality of remedy restriction, as a justification.

rules.rule_statement: When the seller is unable to perform fully under the contract, such as when the amount of land called for in the contract is more than the seller owns, then either the seller or the buyer can seek specific performance of the contract with an abatement of the purchase price to reflect the flawed performance of the contract. While the seller is usually denied such relief when the flaw is material, the buyer is generally entitled to seek specific performance with abatement regardless of the flaw, unless the flaw is so substantial as to call into question whether the parties entered into an agreement at all. For the court to permit this remedy, the amount of the abatement must be ascertainable, such as by using pro rata reduction in the purchase price to reflect the reduction in the size of property transferred.

While the seller is usually denied such relief when the flaw is material, the buyer is generally entitled to seek specific performance with abatement regardless of the flaw, unless the flaw is so substantial as to call into question whether the parties entered into an agreement at all. For the court to permit this remedy, the amount of the abatement must be ascertainable, such as by using pro rata reduction in the purchase price to reflect the reduction in the size of property transferred.

rules.rule_statement: Upon a material breach of a real property contract by a party, the other party may rescind the contract. The nonbreaching party is entitled to restitution of any benefits bestowed on the breaching party (e.g., the buyer’s payment of the purchase price). The breaching party is entitled to offset any benefits enjoyed by the nonbreaching party (e.g., the value of the buyer’s use of the property).

Nonbreaching Party
Upon a material breach of a real property contract by a party, the other party may rescind the contract. The nonbreaching party is entitled to restitution of any benefits bestowed on the breaching party (e.g., the buyer’s payment of the purchase price). The breaching party is entitled to offset any benefits enjoyed by the nonbreaching party (e.g., the value of the buyer’s use of the property).

rules.rule_statement: Generally, a breaching party is not entitled to restitution. However, upon the buyer’s breach of a real property contract that involves installment payments, particularly when such installments are due over an extended time period, the buyer may be entitled to restitution to the extent that the installment payments exceed the seller’s damages (i.e., result in a net benefit to the seller).

rules.source_text:
Breaching Party
Generally, a breaching party is not entitled to restitution. However, upon the buyer’s breach of a real property contract that involves installment payments, particularly when such installments are due over an extended time period, the buyer may be entitled to restitution to the extent that the installment payments exceed the seller’s damages (i.e., result in a net benefit to the seller).

rules.rule_statement: Equitable conversion, while not a remedy, can significantly impact the rights of the parties involved in the sale of real property. Under the equitable-conversion doctrine, after a sales contract is entered into, the seller holds only legal title to the property; equitable title in such property passes to the buyer. Note: While equitable conversion is applicable to a contract for the sale of real property, it may apply to any circumstance when there is a duty to convey property, such as when a personal representative of a decedent’s estate has an obligation under a will to sell property and distribute the proceeds.

Equitable Conversion
Equitable conversion, while not a remedy, can significantly impact the rights of the parties involved in the sale of real property. Under the equitable-conversion doctrine, after a sales contract is entered into, the seller holds only legal title to the property; equitable title in such property passes to the buyer. Note: While equitable conversion is applicable to a contract for the sale of real property, it may apply to any circumstance when there is a duty to convey property, such as when a personal representative of a decedent’s estate has an obligation under a will to sell property and distribute the proceeds.

rules.rule_statement: The seller has an equitable lien against the property (i.e., a vendor’s lien) to secure payment of the purchase price. The buyer who has partially paid the purchase price also has an equitable lien against the property (i.e., a vendee’s lien) to ensure repayment in the event of the seller’s breach.

rules.rule_statement: The risk of loss, such as occasioned by fire, is placed on the buyer. The seller can compel the buyer to purchase the damaged or destroyed property, provided the loss was not the seller’s fault.

Risk Of Loss/Forced Sale
The risk of loss, such as occasioned by fire, is placed on the buyer. The seller can compel the buyer to purchase the damaged or destroyed property, provided the loss was not the seller’s fault.

rules.rule_statement: When the property increases in value or the seller refuses to transfer legal title, the buyer can seek specific performance to compel the seller to convey the property to the buyer.

Compelled Transfer To The Buyer
When the property increases in value or the seller refuses to transfer legal title, the buyer can seek specific performance to compel the seller to convey the property to the buyer.

rules.rule_statement: If the seller dies after the contract is executed but before the property is transferred, then the seller’s heirs or devisees are treated as holding the property in trust for the buyer, to whom they must sell the property. If the seller left a will, then the proceeds from the sale go to the legatees (i.e., persons entitled to the seller’s personal property) rather than the devisees (i.e., persons entitled to the seller’s real property).

Effect Of The Seller’S Death
If the seller dies after the contract is executed but before the property is transferred, then the seller’s heirs or devisees are treated as holding the property in trust for the buyer, to whom they must sell the property. If the seller left a will, then the proceeds from the sale go to the legatees (i.e., persons entitled to the seller’s personal property) rather than the devisees (i.e., persons entitled to the seller’s real property).

rules.rule_statement: Regarding a buyer who dies after the contract is executed but before the property is transferred, the personal representative must pay the purchase price first from any personal assets of the estate, thereby reducing or eliminating any distribution to the legatees. The property itself is then distributed to the buyer’s devisees or heirs.

Effect Of The Buyer’S Death
Regarding a buyer who dies after the contract is executed but before the property is transferred, the personal representative must pay the purchase price first from any personal assets of the estate, thereby reducing or eliminating any distribution to the legatees. The property itself is then distributed to the buyer’s devisees or heirs.

rules.rule_statement: When the parties have entered into an option contract, equitable conversion does not occur until the option has been exercised. Consequently, when the owner of the real property for which the option has been given dies before the option has been exercised, the property passes to the owner’s devisees when there is a will. When the option is subsequently exercised, the devisees may be compelled to sell the property to the option holder but are entitled to the proceeds from the sale.

Option Contract
When the parties have entered into an option contract, equitable conversion does not occur until the option has been exercised. Consequently, when the owner of the real property for which the option has been given dies before the option has been exercised, the property passes to the owner’s devisees when there is a will. When the option is subsequently exercised, the devisees may be compelled to sell the property to the option holder but are entitled to the proceeds from the sale.

rules.rule_statement: When a judgment is entered against the seller of real property after the contract of sale has been executed, the states are about evenly divided as to whether the equitable-conversion doctrine applies to prevent the imposition of a judgment lien on the seller’s property interest. When the judgment is entered against the buyer of real property after the sales contract is executed, the doctrine of equitable conversion is usually applied to permit a judgment lien to attach to the buyer’s property interest.

Judgment Creditor
When a judgment is entered against the seller of real property after the contract of sale has been executed, the states are about evenly divided as to whether the equitable-conversion doctrine applies to prevent the imposition of a judgment lien on the seller’s property interest. When the judgment is entered against the buyer of real property after the sales contract is executed, the doctrine of equitable conversion is usually applied to permit a judgment lien to attach to the buyer’s property interest.

rules.rule_statement: When the risk of loss is on the seller, he is entitled to recover the insurance payment. When the risk of loss is on the buyer, she must provide her own insurance policy against loss; otherwise, the buyer is not protected against loss because she cannot enforce the seller’s insurance policy against the insurance company. R2d Contracts § 373. R3d Restitution § 36, Illus. 6.

rules.rule_statement: Formula Buyer’s damages for seller’s breach = (market price − contract price) + (incidental + consequential damages) − (expenses saved as a consequence of breach) The time of measuring the market price is the time that the buyer learns of the breach. Alternatively, the buyer may buy substitute goods (i.e., cover); such a buyer is entitled to the difference between the replacement cost (rather than the market price) and the contract price. If the buyer accepts nonconforming goods, then the buyer may pursue a breach-of-warranty action; the buyer can recover the difference between the value of the goods accepted and the value of such goods had they been as warranted. Value is determined as of the date of acceptance. Incidental and consequential damages may be recovered as well. If the buyer justifiably revokes acceptance of received goods, then the buyer, in addition to recovering the market–contract differential, may recover any amount paid to the seller for the goods. In addition, the buyer has not only a security interest in any such goods for the amount paid but also the right to sell such goods.

rules.rule_statement: When a seller justifiably withholds delivery of goods because the buyer has breached the contract, the buyer is nevertheless entitled to restitution of any amount by which the buyer’s payments exceed the seller’s liquidated damages under the contract. When the contract does not specify liquidated damages, the seller is entitled to retain the smaller of $500 or 20% of the value of the buyer’s total contractual performance.

rules.rule_statement: Formula Seller’s damages for buyer’s breach = (market price − contract price) + (incidental damages − expenses saved as a consequence of the buyer’s breach). The time of measuring the market price is the time for the seller to tender the goods. If the market–contract differential is not adequate to place the seller in as good a position as performance of the contract would have done, then the seller can seek lost profits. The seller also has the right to resell the goods if the buyer breaches or repudiates the contract. In such a case, the seller can seek damages based on the difference between the resale price (instead of the market price) and the contract price.

rules.rule_statement: When the buyer has accepted the goods, the seller may reclaim the goods under limited circumstances. The buyer must have received the goods on credit, the buyer must have been insolvent at that time, and the seller must act within 10 days after the buyer receives the goods to reclaim the goods. rules.canonical_name: Owner’S Damages For The Builder’S Breach
rules.rule_statement: When a builder has breached a construction contract, the owner is entitled to recover damages. Damages may be based on either the reduction in market value of the building caused by the breach (i.e., value damages) or the cost to complete the building or to remedy any defect (i.e., cost damages). Any special damages suffered by the owner, such as the value of the loss of the use of the building (e.g., its rental value), may also be recovered. When the cost to complete the building is the basis for damages, any cost avoided (e.g., an amount owed to the builder under the contract but unpaid) reduces the amount of the owner’s damages. Cost damages may be denied if the amount is clearly disproportionate to the owner’s loss.

rules.canonical_name: Builder’S Damages For The Owner’S Breach
rules.rule_statement: When an owner has breached a construction contract, the builder is entitled to recover damages. If the builder completes the building before the owner’s breach, then the builder is entitled to maintain an action for the price to recover the amount that the owner still owes under the contract. If the owner breaches before the builder completes the building, then the builder is entitled to his lost profit, which is usually measured by the contract price less the cost of completing the contract. This amount is reduced by any payments made by the owner as well as by the value of any materials that the builder can salvage, such as by using the same materials on another job.

rules.canonical_name: Owner’S Remedy When The Builder Breaches
rules.rule_statement: When the builder fails to perform or totally breaches or repudiates the contract, the owner may rescind the contract and seek restitution of any monies paid to the builder on the contract. Otherwise, the owner is generally denied a restitution remedy because she cannot return the contractual benefits received from the builder.

rules.canonical_name: Builder’S Remedy When The Builder Breaches
rules.rule_statement: When the builder breaches the contract, he may nevertheless rescind the contract and seek restitution of the benefit that he has conferred upon the owner. Generally, the builder is entitled to recover the increase in the market value of the owner’s property due to the builder’s efforts less any loss suffered by the owner because of the builder’s breach. The builder’s recovery is limited to the ratable portion of the contract price. Moreover, the builder’s recovery may be denied when the owner does not enjoy net benefit as measured against the contract price.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, an owner cannot utilize specific performance to compel a builder to construct a building under a contract. The legal remedy of damages usually provides the owner adequate resources with which to build the desired structure. In addition, courts have been reluctant to order specific performance when extensive supervision is required. However, a plaintiff may seek specific performance if the building is to be constructed on land owned by the builder-defendant. R2d Contracts § 348(1), (2). R2d Contracts § 374; Illus. 2; R3d Restitution § 36, Illus. 1. R2d Contracts §§ 371, Illus. 1, 373.

rules.canonical_name: Employee’S Damages For An Employer’S Breach
rules.rule_statement: Formula Employee’s damages for breach of employment contract = (total compensation + fringe benefits for the contract term) + (incidental + consequential damages) − (avoidable consequences) Avoidable consequences include any income that the employee earned or could have earned elsewhere during the term of the contract. In determining the avoidable-consequences reduction for attributable income, the employee must use reasonable diligence in looking for substitute employment, but the substitute employment must be comparable to the contractual employment. In addition, because the avoidable-consequences rule is an affirmative defense, the employer has the burden of proof as to the availability of substitute employment as well as the amount of income from it. Some states permit an employee to pursue a tort action for wrongful discharge against an employer when the discharge violates public policy. In a successful action, the employee may recover as damages any lost income as well as emotional distress damages and punitive damages.

rules.rule_statement: An employee can rescind an employment contract that the employer has breached and recover under a restitutionary money judgment action if the employer receives an unjust benefit because of the employee’s efforts. The employee is entitled to recover the reasonable value of the employee’s services. The contract price is evidence but not conclusive evidence of this value. An employee can also rescind an employment contract that the employee has breached and recover under a restitutionary money judgment action. The employee’s recovery is limited to the benefit conferred on the employer less any loss caused by the employee’s breach. When the employee’s actions have not benefited the employer, such as when the employee’s breach results in an unfinished project that the employer cannot find someone else to complete, then the employee cannot recover.

rules.rule_statement: An employer can rescind an employment contract that the employee has breached and recover under a restitutionary money judgment action when the employee receives an unjust benefit due to the employer’s efforts. For example, the employer can seek the return of advance compensation paid to the employee that the employee had not earned at the time of the breach. An employer can also rescind an employment contract that the employer has breached and recover under a restitutionary money judgment action. The employer’s recovery is limited to the benefit conferred on the employee (e.g., advance compensation payments) less any loss incurred by the employee because of the employer’s breach.

rules.rule_statement: A promise to render a personal service is not specifically enforceable by the employer. When the service is not unique, damages are adequate to enable the employer to secure performance of the same service by someone else. Even if the service is unique and damages would therefore not be adequate to secure alternative performance by someone else, a court will not order specific performance because requiring performance would constitute involuntary servitude and fly in the face of public policy.

rules.rule_statement: An employer may seek an injunction against a former employee to enforce a covenant not to compete. (The buyer of a business may also enforce a noncompete agreement against the seller of the business through an injunction.) To enforce a covenant not to compete, the covenant must be reasonable in its temporal and geographic scope, and it must be no broader than is necessary to protect the employer (or buyer). If the covenant is too broad, then some courts will refuse to enforce it; other courts will strike out the offending language (i.e., blue pencil) and preserve the remainder of the parties’ agreement. EXAM NOTE: While California prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind (with very narrow exceptions), application and knowledge of California law are not required to answer a remedies question. rules.rule_statement: When an oral contract is unenforceable because of the Statute of Frauds (e.g., a contract for the sale of land or to render services for more than one year), the plaintiff is generally not entitled to recover damages. When the defendant cannot assert the Statute of Frauds as a defense because of estoppel, the plaintiff can recover damages, although recovery may be limited to reliance damages, and damages may be denied when restitution provides an adequate remedy.

rules.rule_statement: When an oral contract is unenforceable because of the Statute of Frauds, the plaintiff usually can recover through a restitutionary money purchase action any benefit conferred on the defendant in reliance on the unenforceable agreement. Amounts expended by the plaintiff in reliance on the contract that do not produce a benefit for the defendant cannot be recovered. When the benefit consists of money paid by the plaintiff to the defendant, the plaintiff is entitled to the return of the amount paid. Otherwise, the measure of the benefit is the greater of either the market value of the plaintiff’s performance or the increase in the defendant’s wealth.

rules.rule_statement: When an oral contract for the transfer of land is unenforceable because of the Statute of Frauds, the plaintiff can obtain specific performance if there has been part performance of the contract. Typically, part performance requires the plaintiff to undertake at least two of the following actions unless the defendant admits the existence of the contract: (1) partial payment of the purchase price, (2) possession of the property, and (3) improvements made to the property.

Typically, part performance requires the plaintiff to undertake at least two of the following actions unless the defendant admits the existence of the contract: (1) partial payment of the purchase price, (2) possession of the property, and (3) improvements made to the property.

rules.rule_statement: Rule When a party’s contractual duty is discharged due to impracticability of performance or frustration of purpose, the plaintiff usually can recover through a restitutionary money judgment action any benefit conferred on the defendant in reliance on or performance of the agreement. When the benefit consists of money paid by the plaintiff to the defendant, the plaintiff is entitled to the return of the amount paid. Otherwise, the measure of the benefit is usually the market value of the plaintiff’s performance. When the contract price can be apportioned between the work finished and the work uncompleted, the contract price apportioned to the work finished may serve as a ceiling on the plaintiff’s recovery. Generally, a party’s reliance damages cannot be recovered or offset against the other party’s recovery.

rules.rule_statement: A minor may disaffirm a contract that he entered into as a minor. An adult may disaffirm a contract that she entered into while a minor, provided that the adult does so within a reasonable time after becoming an adult. Similarly, a person lacking mental capacity who enters into a contract may disaffirm the contract. Note: A contract entered into by a person for whom a guardian has been appointed because of a lack of mental capacity is void, not merely voidable.

A minor may disaffirm a contract that he entered into as a minor. rules.rule_statement: Generally, when a contract is disaffirmed by a minor or a person lacking mental capacity, the minor or person lacking mental capacity is entitled to receive full restitution for whatever benefit was bestowed on the other party to the contract. The other party, however, is entitled to full restitution only regarding any benefit bestowed on a person lacking mental capacity if the other party was unaware of the incapacity. Otherwise, the other party is entitled to restitution of any benefit that the minor or person lacking mental capacity still possesses. For example, if the other party sold a car to a minor and the car was subsequently damaged, then the other party is entitled to recover only the damaged car. A person who furnishes necessities (e.g., food, clothing) to a minor or a person lacking mental capacity is entitled to recover the full value of such necessities in a restitutionary money judgment action, even if the necessities have been consumed, as is often the case. However, the contract price serves as a ceiling on the amount that can be recovered. Contrary to the general rule, a minor may seek restitution of a benefit even if the benefit has been transferred to a bona fide purchaser.

rules.rule_statement: When a contract is unenforceable because of illegality, the general rule is that damages, specific performance, and restitution are denied. However, a party is entitled to restitution despite the contract being unenforceable if: i) Denial of restitution would cause disproportionate forfeiture (e.g., a foreign corporation that enters into a contract despite its failure to appoint a local agent for service of process); ii) Denial of restitution would leave property in the hands of a person whose control of it would be contrary to public policy (e.g., the stakeholder of an illegal betting agreement); iii) The party seeking restitution is excusably ignorant of the facts; iv) The party seeking restitution is not equally wrong with the other party, such as when one party takes advantage of a position of trust; or v) The party seeking restitution did not engage in serious misconduct and withdrew from the transaction before its improper purpose was achieved. rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff who makes a payment of money to a person who was not the intended recipient or makes a payment that was not intended can seek restitution. Similarly, a plaintiff who makes a monetary payment that results from a mistake as to the existence or extent of the plaintiff’s obligation to the recipient can seek restitution to the extent that the payment was not due. The plaintiff’s negligence in making such a payment does not usually prevent the plaintiff from obtaining restitution. Under the voluntary payment rule, money that is voluntarily paid despite a recognized uncertainty as to the existence or extent of the payor’s obligation to the recipient cannot be recovered on the basis of mistake when the payor subsequently learns that the payment exceeds the actual amount of the payor’s obligation.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff who makes a payment in discharge of a lien due to mistake (e.g., mistake as to ownership of property subject to the lien) may be subrogated to the discharged lien to the extent necessary to prevent unjust enrichment of the defendant. A similar remedy is provided to a lender who advances funds to a borrower to discharge a lien but is mistaken as to the effect of the discharge, such as when the lender is unaware of intervening interests.

rules.rule_statement: When a plaintiff-donor has transferred as a gift something more than or different from the property intended to be transferred or the plaintiff-donor’s gift is made to someone other than the intended donee, the plaintiff can seek restitution to prevent the unjust enrichment of the defendant-recipient. When real property has been transferred, the plaintiff can seek cancellation or reformation of the deed to reflect the plaintiff’s donative intent. When personal property has been transferred, the plaintiff can seek specific restitution of the excess or different property. The plaintiff-donor who has made a mistake that induced a gift to the defendant may seek restitution. The mistake must be a misapprehension of circumstances that exist at the time of the gift, and that misapprehension must be the cause in fact of the gift. Misapprehension of the donee’s identity, the donee’s relationship to the donor, or the value of the property transferred can give rise to restitution; the donor’s disappointed expectations or errors in judgment do not constitute grounds for restitution. When a donor attempts to make a gift before dying or becoming incapacitated while believing that the gift was perfected but, because of a mistake, the attempted gift was partly or wholly ineffective, the property transferred was something other than what was intended, or the property was transferred to someone other than the intended recipient, then the intended recipient can seek restitution to prevent the unjust enrichment of the unintended recipient of the attempted gift or of the heirs or other successors in interest of the donor. When real property is transferred, the intended recipient can seek reformation of the deed to reflect the donor’s intent or a constructive trust on the property held by the unintended recipient. rules.rule_statement: Unconscionability has traditionally functioned as an equitable defense in a contract action when specific performance is sought. Unconscionability
Unconscionability has traditionally functioned as an equitable defense in a contract action when specific performance is sought. While retaining this function, unconscionability, which has both procedural and substantive roots, now also serves as the basis for remedies, albeit usually applied defensively.

rules.rule_statement: Unconscionability is most frequently found in transactions involving consumers. For example, a contract provision that limits consequential damages for personal injuries is prima facie unconscionable in a consumer goods transaction. However, unconscionability is not confined to consumer dealings. For example, a contract provision that eliminates reasonable notification of a contract’s termination can be unconscionable.

Scope Of Protection
Unconscionability is most frequently found in transactions involving consumers. For example, a contract provision that limits consequential damages for personal injuries is prima facie unconscionable in a consumer goods transaction. However, unconscionability is not confined to consumer dealings. For example, a contract provision that eliminates reasonable notification of a contract’s termination can be unconscionable.
